title: Determination of lease — how a tenancy legally ends and eviction procedure
text: Under Section 111 of the Transfer of Property Act 1882, a lease of immovable property determines or comes to an end in the following ways: by efflux of time limited by the lease, by the happening of an event agreed upon, by the lessee acquiring the property, by the lessee renouncing his character as such, by the forfeiture of the lease, by the expiry of notice to quit, or by the lessee accepting a surrender of the lease. A landlord cannot evict a tenant by force, by removing the tenant's belongings, by changing locks, by cutting off utilities, or by any form of harassment or intimidation. The only legal method of eviction is through a court order. The landlord must file a suit for eviction in the appropriate civil court or Rent Control Court. The court will issue a summons to the tenant, hear both sides, and only then issue an eviction order if the legal grounds are satisfied. A tenant who receives a notice to quit has the right to contest the eviction in court. The tenant can remain in the property until the court issues an order directing them to vacate. Forcible eviction without a court order is a criminal offence and the tenant can file a complaint with the police and approach the magistrate for relief. The tenant's right to contest eviction exists even if there is no written lease agreement, even if the tenant has defaulted on rent, and even if the landlord wishes to sell the property.
